:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Misdemeanants with an aggregated sentence of over 60 days and felons are eligible for parole. However, the method of determining release eligibility varies depending on whether an offender is sentenced to state prison (for a sentence of over two and a half years) or to a House of Correction (for a sentence of under two and a half years). Minimum sentences are set by the court, with some limitations. State prison sentences must be at minimum one year in length. For those sentenced to life in prison but given the possibility of parole, judges may set a minimum sentence of between 15 and 25 years. The maximum term is also set by the court, but may not exceed the longest statutory punishment for the crime of conviction. Good conduct credits earned for work or education reduce both the minimum term, (for purposes of parole eligibility calculations), and the maximum term, (for purposes of reducing the total term to be served in prison). In general, good conduct credits may reduce a sentence by up to one-third, or 10 days per month.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 1,856 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 26% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 37% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 95% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 35-44 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 29% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Predicted Probabilities

Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date.
